What History, Economics, and Technology Tell Us About the Future Price of Artificial Intelligence Use

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image from PexelsG’day Folks
Happy weekend! After watching technology evolve for half a century, I have learned one valuable lesson: revolutionary innovations do not fail because of poor technology, but they struggle because of economics. I will explain this in this short story.
When I first heard someone compare artificial intelligence to Uber, I almost dismissed the idea as another catchy headline designed to attract clicks. After all, what could a ride-sharing company possibly have in common with Co-Pilot, ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or Grok?
As it turns out, perhaps more than I initially thought. Not because AI companies will become taxi services, but because both industries share an important economic challenge. During their early years, they relied heavily on investor funding to grow rapidly, attract loyal users, and establish market leadership. For customers, it felt almost too good to be true.
Many of us remember when Uber rides seemed unbelievably inexpensive. The prices encouraged people to abandon traditional taxis and embrace a new way of traveling. Eventually, however, the economics began to change. Investors expected…
